errors.listing.interrupted.title: Interrupted
errors.listing.interrupted.explanation: A system operation was interrupted before it could finish. This is almost always a one-off, caused by a signal or background process momentarily getting in the way.
errors.listing.interrupted.suggestion: Navigate here again to retry. This kind of interruption almost never happens twice in a row.

errors.listing.notEnoughMemory.title: Not enough memory
errors.listing.notEnoughMemory.explanati: The system ran out of available memory (RAM) while reading this folder. This can happen when many apps are open at once, or when a folder contains a very large number of files.
errors.listing.notEnoughMemory.suggestio:
Here's what to try:
- Close some apps to free up memory, especially ones using lots of resources (browsers with many tabs, editors, media apps)
- Check memory usage in **Activity Monitor** (search for it in Spotlight)
- Navigate here again to retry

errors.listing.resourceBusy.title: Resource busy
errors.listing.resourceBusy.explanation: Cmdr couldn't access `{path}` because another app or process is currently using it exclusively. This is usually temporary.
errors.listing.resourceBusy.suggestion: Wait a moment, then navigate here again. If it keeps happening, check which app might be holding the file open (in Terminal, run `lsof +D <folder-path>` to see which processes are using this folder).

errors.listing.networkDown.title: Network is down
errors.listing.networkDown.explanation: Your Mac's network connection is down, so Cmdr can't reach this volume. This could mean Wi-Fi is disconnected, an Ethernet cable is unplugged, or the network interface is disabled.
errors.listing.networkDown.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Check Wi-Fi or Ethernet status in **{system_settings} > Network**
- If you're on Wi-Fi, try turning it off and on again
- In Terminal, run `ping google.com` to test your connection
- Navigate here again once you're back online

errors.listing.hostDown.title: Host is down
errors.listing.hostDown.explanation: The remote host (the computer or server hosting this volume) isn't responding. It may be powered off, sleeping, or temporarily unreachable.
errors.listing.hostDown.suggestion:

- Check that the host is powered on and connected to the network
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if it's reachable
- If it's a NAS or server, check its management interface
- Navigate here again once the host is back

errors.listing.staleConnection.title: Stale connection
errors.listing.staleConnection.explanati: Cmdr is trying to access this folder using an old reference that the server no longer recognizes. This commonly happens with network drives (NFS, SMB) after the server restarts, the share is remounted, or the connection was interrupted.
errors.listing.staleConnection.suggestio:
Here's what to try:
- Navigate away from this folder and come back
- If this is a network drive, try unmounting and remounting it in Finder
- Check that the server hosting this folder is running
- In Terminal, run `mount` to see currently mounted volumes

errors.listing.lockUnavailable.title: Lock unavailable
errors.listing.lockUnavailable.explanati: The system ran out of file locks. File locks are how apps coordinate access to shared files (preventing two apps from writing to the same file at once). Running out usually means too many apps are accessing files simultaneously.
errors.listing.lockUnavailable.suggestio:
Here's what to try:
- Close some apps, especially ones that work with many files (editors, IDEs, backup tools)
- In Terminal, run `lsof | wc -l` to see how many files are open across all apps
- If the problem keeps happening, you can raise the limit with `ulimit -n 4096` in Terminal
- Navigate here again to retry

errors.listing.notPermitted.title: Not permitted
errors.listing.notPermitted.explanation: macOS blocked Cmdr from accessing `{path}`. This usually means the folder is protected by macOS security policies, or Cmdr hasn't been granted the right permissions yet.
errors.listing.notPermitted.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Open **{system_settings} > {privacy_and_security} > {files_and_folders}** and grant Cmdr access
- If this is a system-protected folder (like system directories), you may need to grant Cmdr **{full_disk_access}** under {privacy_and_security}
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to check ownership and permissions

errors.listing.pathNotFoundErrno.title: Path not found
errors.listing.pathNotFoundErrno.explana: Cmdr couldn't find `{path}`. It may have been moved, renamed, or deleted while Cmdr was trying to access it.
errors.listing.pathNotFoundErrno.suggest:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the path is spelled correctly
- If this is on a network drive, make sure it's connected and the share is accessible
- Navigate to the parent folder and look for the item there
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on the parent folder to see what's there

errors.listing.noPermissionErrno.title: No permission
errors.listing.noPermissionErrno.explana: Cmdr doesn't have permission to access `{path}`. macOS controls which apps can access which folders, and Cmdr hasn't been granted access to this one yet.
errors.listing.noPermissionErrno.suggest:
Here's what to try:
- Open **{system_settings} > {privacy_and_security} > {files_and_folders}** and grant Cmdr access
- Check the folder's permissions in Finder: right-click it, choose Get Info, and look under Sharing & Permissions
- If this is a shared folder, ask the owner to update permissions
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to see the current permissions

errors.listing.diskFullErrno.title: Disk is full
errors.listing.diskFullErrno.explanation: There isn't enough free space on this volume to complete the operation.
errors.listing.diskFullErrno.suggestion:
Here's what to try:
- Free up space by moving or deleting files you no longer need
- Empty the Trash (right-click the Trash icon in the Dock)
- In Terminal, run `df -h` to see how much space is left on each volume
- Check **{system_settings} > General > Storage** for a breakdown of what's using space

errors.listing.readOnlyVolumeErrno.title: Read-only volume
errors.listing.readOnlyVolumeErrno.expla: This volume is mounted as read-only, so Cmdr can't make changes to it. This could be because the device has a physical write-protection switch, the disk image was mounted read-only, or the file system doesn't support writing.
errors.listing.readOnlyVolumeErrno.sugge:
Here's what to try:
- If the device has a physical write-protection switch (common on SD cards), flip it off
- If this is a disk image, remount it with write access
- Otherwise, copy the files to a writable location first

errors.listing.networkUnreachable.title: Network unreachable
errors.listing.networkUnreachable.explan: Cmdr can't reach the network this volume is on. This often means you're not connected to the right network, or a VPN isn't active.
errors.listing.networkUnreachable.sugges:

- Make sure you're on the right network (for example, your office Wi-Fi or VPN)
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test if the server is reachable
- Navigate here again once you're connected

errors.listing.connectionRefused.title: Connection refused
errors.listing.connectionRefused.explana: The server actively refused the connection. This usually means the server software (for example, an SMB or NFS service) isn't running, or it's configured to reject connections from this Mac.
errors.listing.connectionRefused.suggest:

- Check that the server is running and its file sharing service is active
- Verify the server address and port are correct
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to check if the server is reachable at all
- Navigate here again to retry

errors.listing.symlinkLoopErrno.title: Symlink loop
errors.listing.symlinkLoopErrno.explanat: Cmdr found a circular chain of symbolic links (shortcuts that point to other shortcuts) at `{path}`. Following these links leads in a circle, so Cmdr can't reach the actual file or folder.
errors.listing.symlinkLoopErrno.suggesti:
Here's what to try:
- In Terminal, run `ls -la` on this path to see where the symbolic links point
- Find and fix the link that creates the loop
- If you're not sure which link is the problem, follow them one by one with `readlink <path>`

errors.listing.nameTooLongErrno.title: Name too long
errors.listing.nameTooLongErrno.explanat: The file or folder name exceeds the system's limit (255 characters on most Mac volumes). This can also happen when the full path (all folders combined) exceeds the system's maximum path length.
errors.listing.nameTooLongErrno.suggesti: Rename the item to use a shorter name. If the name looks reasonable, the full path (including all parent folders) might be too long. Try moving it to a shorter path.

errors.listing.hostUnreachable.title: Host unreachable
errors.listing.hostUnreachable.explanati: Cmdr can't find a network route to the host this volume is on. This usually means the host is on a different network, behind a firewall, or the routing configuration needs updating.
errors.listing.hostUnreachable.suggestio:

- Check that the host is powered on and on the same network
- If you need a VPN to reach it, make sure the VPN is connected
- In Terminal, try `ping <hostname>` to test connectivity
- Navigate here again once the host is reachable

errors.listing.diskReadProblem.title: Disk read problem
errors.listing.diskReadProblem.explanati: Cmdr hit a hardware-level read problem at `{path}`. This means the disk or device had trouble reading the data, which could be a temporary glitch or a sign of a failing disk.
errors.listing.diskReadProblem.suggestio:
Here's what to try:
- Check that the disk or device is still properly connected
- Open **Disk Utility** (search for it in Spotlight) and run **First Aid** on this volume
- If this keeps happening, back up your data as soon as possible. The disk may be developing bad sectors or starting to wear out.

errors.listing.tccRestricted.title: This folder is restricted by macOS
errors.listing.tccRestricted.explanation: Cmdr can't read `{path}` because macOS hasn't granted access to this folder yet. This is a privacy gate, not a Cmdr bug.
errors.listing.tccRestricted.suggestion:
Two ways to fix:
- Grant Cmdr **{full_disk_access}** in **{system_settings} → {privacy_and_security} → {full_disk_access}** to remove all such limits at once.
- Or grant per-folder access for just this folder in **{system_settings} → {privacy_and_security} → {files_and_folders} → Cmdr**.

errors.listing.emptyRootICloud.title: iCloud Drive looks empty
errors.listing.emptyRootICloud.explanati:
Cmdr opened iCloud Drive but it came back with no files. macOS hides iCloud Drive contents from apps that don't have **{full_disk_access}**, so granting Cmdr that permission is the most likely fix.

If your iCloud Drive really is empty, you can ignore this hint.
errors.listing.emptyRootICloud.suggestio:
Here's what to try:
- Open [**{system_settings} > {privacy_and_security}**](x-apple.systempreferences:com.apple.preference.security?Privacy) and pick **{full_disk_access}**
- Add Cmdr (use the **+** button) and toggle it on
- Quit and reopen Cmdr
- Come back here to retry
